summary.category: Britain's Industrial Revolution: Origins and Key Innovations
points: Industrialisation began in late 18th-century Britain due to: accumulated colonial capital, domestic market from enclosure, technological innovations, abundant coal, access to raw materials from colonies, and merchant class dominance over aristocracy.

Textile innovations transformed production: flying shuttle (1733) increased weaving speed; spinning jenny (1764) multiplied spinners; water frame (1769) mechanised spinning; power loom (1785) mechanised weaving—each innovation created unemployment while increasing production.
James Watt's steam engine improvements enabled factories independent of water power, concentrated production in cities, and powered interconnected industrial systems (railways, mines, factories).

Steam-powered factories concentrated hundreds or thousands of workers in single locations, marked revolutionary shift in labour organisation from dispersed craft production to centralised factory discipline.

summary.category: The Factory System and Working-Class Experience
points: Factory system involved strict hierarchy and discipline: machines determined pace (not workers), repetitive deskilled tasks replaced craft skill, management held complete control, lateness and insubordination were punished.

Working conditions were brutal: 12–16 hour workdays, dangerous unguarded machinery causing injuries/amputations with no compensation, child labour (ages 5–12) at 40–50% of workforce, diseases from pollution, minimal subsistence wages.

Luddite movements (1811–1817) represented skilled workers resisting displacement through machine-breaking; government suppressed violently, making machine-breaking capital crime, executing and transporting Luddites to Australia.

Factory concentration enabled collective worker action: early combinations (unions) formed, held strikes, demanded wage/condition improvements; governments and employers suppressed unions violently but worker organisation persisted.
Industrialisation caused massive migration to cities: Manchester grew from 25,000 (1772) to 300,000+ (1840); industrial cities housed factories and became centres of production and commerce.

Working-class urban neighbourhoods were crowded, unsanitary tenements with families in single rooms, no proper sanitation, leading to cholera/typhoid epidemics, extremely high infant mortality, contrasting sharply with middle-class areas.

Industrialisation created new middle class of manufacturers and merchants accumulating enormous wealth; they became politically powerful, promoted free trade, challenged aristocratic dominance, and promoted ideologies of hard work, morality, and progress.

Factory concentration created unprecedented working-class consciousness: millions shared common conditions enabling recognition of shared class interests opposed to capitalists; socialism developed as working-class alternative ideology.

summary.category: Global Impacts: Colonial Subordination and Destruction of Traditional Industries
points: British industrialisation depended on global resources: American enslaved-labour cotton supplied textile mills; colonies provided raw materials; British manufactured goods destroyed local industries worldwide.

British colonial policies deliberately de-industrialised India: destroyed textile industry (employing millions), imposed tariffs making Indian goods uncompetitive in Indian markets, promoted cash crops, forced India into raw material exporter dependent on British imports.

British goods flooded global markets underselling Indian textiles, Ottoman goods, Chinese manufactures; wherever British goods could reach, local manufacturers were destroyed; industrialised Britain dominated non-industrialised regions politically and economically.

Colonial subordination to industrial powers: colonies supplied raw materials at low prices and purchased expensive British manufactures, creating wealth transfer from colonies to Britain and establishing global hierarchies of industrial vs. non-industrial nations.

Industrialisation presented as progress and civilisation; factory production increased goods, improved transport (railways, steamships), created wealth; pre-industrial societies portrayed as primitive—yet ideology obscured social costs and colonial dependence.

Free market ideology (Adam Smith, David Ricardo) argued unrestricted markets naturally produced best outcomes; governments shouldn't regulate; individuals pursuing profit served public good; justified minimal worker protections and colonialism.

Socialism and communism developed as working-class alternatives: Marx argued capitalism inevitably created class conflict leading to worker revolution; other socialists proposed reforms (wages, hours, healthcare); governments suppressed socialism violently.

Child labour was justified as necessary for family survival while being structural to industrial profits; women's factory employment contradicted 'separate spheres' ideology but enabled super-exploitation (30–50% lower wages, unpaid domestic labour).

summary.category: Environmental and Continuing Spread of Industrialisation
points: Industrialisation created unprecedented environmental damage: coal smoke and air pollution in industrial cities, river pollution from industrial waste, deforestation, mining landscape destruction, pesticide contamination; costs were externalised onto workers and communities.

Industrialisation spread to continental Europe (France, Germany, Belgium) and North America (USA) by mid-19th century; many countries used state support for industrial development; Germany and USA emerged competing with Britain by late 19th century.

Industrialised nations competed for colonies and markets driving late 19th-century imperialism; industrial military technology gave overwhelming superiority enabling conquests; industrial military competition contributed to World War I arms races and unprecedented industrialised warfare.
